video_title: Rory Johnston on Why His $200 Oil Prediction Didn't Turn Out Right | Odd Lots
gist: Oil analyst Rory Johnston does a post-mortem on why his $150-$200 Brent call failed during the 2026 Iran war / Strait of Hormuz closure (Brent peaked just shy of $120 and is now ~$74 in contango). The single biggest swing factor was China cutting crude imports by ~5 million b/d and silently releasing reserves, which absorbed half the Asian supply shock; secondary factors were IEA SPR releases and Trump-driven downside volatility that crippled traders' risk limits even as their models screamed 'buy'.

insights.claim: The Strait of Hormuz closure during the 2026 Iran war was the largest supply disruption in oil market history, shutting in ~13 million b/d (over 13% of global supply) net of all known rerouting via the Saudi East-West and Emirati Fujairah pipelines.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Establishes the magnitude of the shock the market absorbed without a price spike, reframing how investors should size future Hormuz tail-risk scenarios.
insights.quote: we were still down roughly 13 million barrels a day of Gulf oil production excluding Iran that had been forcibly shut in through the duration of this crisis

insights.claim: The $200/barrel target was the price at which Rory Johnston expected demand destruction to clear a 13 million b/d supply hole with no further offsets, a depth of demand destruction never previously observed.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Shows the demand-destruction pricing model is sound but that hidden inventory buffers can substitute for it, breaking the model's predictive power in practice.
insights.quote: when you have a supply shock that large without any more offsets, you end up at demand destructive pricing really, really fast

insights.claim: Brent peaked just shy of $120 twice (late March and late April 2026) and has since collapsed to below $74, with prompt time spreads flipping into outright contango on the recording day.
insights.speaker: Joe Weisenthal / Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Contango plus sub-$74 pricing signals the market is now oversupplied at the margin despite the war, a bearish structural setup.
insights.quote: not only are crude oil prices collapsing, but we've actually seen prompt Brent time spreads, the difference between the first and the second contract, actually flip into outright contango today

insights.claim: The combination of three factors — Chinese import contraction, IEA SPR releases, and Trump-driven downside volatility — was far more powerful than any single factor and proved the oil market is much more resilient to large shocks than previously appreciated.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Investors should lower the implied price impact they assign to future supply shocks, including hypothetical Hormuz or Taiwan disruptions.
insights.quote: the oil market is much more resilient, at least in this kind of condition, to a big shock like that in a way that I just hadn't appreciated at the time

insights.claim: Chinese crude oil imports fell by upwards of 5 million b/d from the pre-war three-month average through June 2026, accounting for over half of the total spot-market supply hit to Asia.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: China's import cut, not OPEC or the US, was the dominant balancing mechanism; tracking its return to the market is the key swing variable for oil prices.
insights.quote: Chinese oil imports, crude oil imports into China, the world's largest crude oil importer, fell by upwards of 5 million barrels a day

insights.claim: By absorbing the shock itself, China stopped competing for spot barrels, allowing South Korea, Japan, Australia and Taiwan to recover imports back to pre-war levels by May-June after collapsing in March-April.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Explains the disconnect between acute physical shortage in Asian importers and the absence of a sustained price spike.
insights.quote: the largest facilitator of that was the fact that China was not competing for any of the other barrels, and it then absorbed so much of the shock itself

insights.claim: China's apparent demand destruction during the crisis matched the steepest on record and rivaled the 2022 COVID-zero shock in volume, yet mobility indicators showed no notable decline, implying the swing was reserve releases and substitution rather than genuine demand loss.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: If the 'demand' was actually stockpile drawdown, it is non-recurring and reverses sharply when China restocks, a latent bullish catalyst.
insights.quote: The implied demand destruction we saw through this crisis was on par with the steepest demand destruction implied in history, and on par on the volume with the COVID zero demand shock in 2022

insights.claim: Chinese petrol prices in Beijing rose only ~30% versus a global doubling because Beijing throttled domestic retail prices through regulatory procedures, undermining the case that price-driven demand destruction explains the import collapse.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Without a domestic price signal, China's import cut must be supply-side (reserves), reinforcing that it is reversible.
insights.quote: Prices of petrol in in Beijing only rose maybe 30% versus the doubling we saw globally

insights.claim: Satellite-visible Chinese crude floating-roof tanks remained near pre-crisis highs, but China's six known SPR storage caverns (~131 million barrels) and an estimated billion-plus barrels of untrackable refined-product stocks were likely drawn down silently to bridge the gap.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: The opacity of Chinese underground caverns and product stocks means the market is flying blind on the size of the buffer that must eventually be refilled.
insights.quote: they have six storage caverns at least that we know of about 131 million barrels. The likelihood is that they have been drawing down those

insights.claim: Chinese crude refining run rates fell 3 to 3.5 million b/d during the crisis, and because crude imports fell faster than run rates, the residual was almost certainly covered by silent crude inventory releases.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: The run-rate cut implies real refined-product stock drawdowns that must reverse, supporting a delayed demand rebound thesis.
insights.quote: crude refining run rates also fell dramatically, by 3 to 3 and 1/2 million barrels a day

insights.claim: China's EV penetration cannot explain the oil import drop because fuel demand is driven by fleet composition, not sales mix, and China's vehicle stock is still roughly 10-to-1 internal combustion engines to EVs despite EVs being ~60% of new sales.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Debunks the structural-EV explanation, keeping near-term Chinese oil demand intact and the import cut reversible.
insights.quote: while new energy vehicles in China are like 60% of new vehicle sales, the overall stock of vehicles in China is still 10 to 1 internal combustion engines to EVs

insights.claim: Johnston's 2023 'Chinese oil demand doubts' thesis — that China was secretly building refined-product stocks possibly in anticipation of a Taiwan conflict — was validated by how those reserves cushioned the 2026 Hormuz shock.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Strategic stockpiling for war contingency is a recurring, hard-to-observe driver of Chinese apparent demand that distorts global balance estimates.
insights.quote: back in 2023, I wrote a piece called Chinese oil demand doubts... the worry was that it was in anticipation of a war or an invasion of Taiwan

insights.claim: The biggest unanswered question is why China silently cut imports without any announcement, with candidate explanations being altruistic regional goodwill, protecting its only alternative export markets (Asia/Europe), or a covert US-China deal tied to Washington redeploying military assets from Asia to the Middle East.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Without understanding the motive, the timing of China's return as a competitive importer — a 5 million b/d bullish catalyst — is impossible to handicap.
insights.quote: it remains a mystery as to why China is doing this... it makes it difficult to handicap when they're going to enter back into the market

insights.claim: Jeff Currie's framing that the price response was 'inventory responding to price, not supply responding to price' is the key distinction, and inventory unlike production has a hard floor.
insights.speaker: Tracy Alloway (citing Jeff Currie)
insights.so_what: Once inventories hit tank bottoms, the buffer vanishes and prices can spike violently — the buffer that saved the market this time is finite.
insights.quote: this is not supply responding to price, it's inventory responding to price and inventory, unlike production, has a floor

insights.claim: Johnston models SPRs not as inventory but as discretionary supply/demand, with an SPR release manifesting as 'new supply, not new production' while commercial merchant inventories are what catch the residual surplus/deficit that drives marginal pricing.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Separating strategic from commercial stocks clarifies which inventory moves actually drive price and which are policy-driven supply injections.
insights.quote: an SPR release as an example manifests in the market as new supply, not new production, but new supply that wasn't available before

insights.claim: Cumulative shut-in Middle East production reached ~1.3 billion barrels, dwarfing the IEA SPR release (~400 million barrels) and the Chinese import reduction (~400-500 million barrels), meaning China swung as much or more than all IEA member states combined.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Quantifies that China alone matched the entire developed-world strategic response, underscoring its singular importance to the balance.
insights.quote: China swung as much and likely more than the collective IEA member states

insights.claim: Over 100 million barrels of oil were trapped in Hormuz during the closure and are now exiting at an unsustainable pace, effectively acting as a one-off stockpile release running up against weak Chinese import demand and pushing prices into contango.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: The current contango is partly a transient flush of trapped barrels, so the bearish signal may fade once the backlog clears.
insights.quote: we've had well over 100 million barrels of oil trapped in Hormuz... that is the stockpile, the floating oil available in Hormuz is drawing down faster than it is being replenished

insights.claim: Because the market entered the crisis with over a year of surplus and an even-more-oversupplied forward outlook, traders rationally discounted low inventory levels, knowing stocks would be 'swollen again next year' once Hormuz reopened.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: A glutted forward curve neutralizes the bullish impact of acute drawdowns — the surplus context matters as much as the shock.
insights.quote: it doesn't really matter if they go low because we know they're going to get swollen again next year as soon as this is open

themes.theme: Trump, Jawboning and Trader Risk Limits

insights.claim: Prop oil and product traders' models flagged the market as 'a raging buy' throughout the crisis, but repeated blowouts in March-April forced their risk-management limits down by ~90%, producing a market where 'everyone's bullish but no one's buying.'
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Microstructure and risk-limit constraints, not fundamentals, can suppress a fundamentally justified price melt-up — a key non-obvious driver.
insights.quote: everyone's bullish but no one's buying

insights.claim: Trump-driven downside volatility was a deeply underappreciated factor that injected enough fear to arrest the normal price melt-up by repeatedly 'resetting the clock' on traders even as China's import cuts loosened the physical market.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Policy-induced volatility can be a price-suppressing force in its own right, a variable to weight in future geopolitical oil scenarios.
insights.quote: the potential power of Trump himself, more broadly Trump administration, to inject so much downside volatility that it kind of arrested the normal melt-up process

insights.claim: Johnston believes neither China's import contraction alone nor Trump's jawboning alone would have been sufficient to prevent the melt-up, but their combination proved especially potent.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Highlights that the dampening was a fragile coincidence of two forces, not a robust new equilibrium — the next shock may play out differently.
insights.quote: the combination of them proved to be way more powerful than I have ever expected

insights.claim: A non-trivial share of the $10 Brent rise from ~$60 at the start of 2026 to ~$70 on the eve of war was a precautionary geopolitical war premium, but prompt Brent dipping into contango in the final pre-war week signaled the market was already oversupplied.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Contango is a reliable real-time tell of oversupply; its reappearance now suggests the post-war floor may be lower than the pre-war $70.
insights.quote: on the eve of the war, the last week before the war, we saw prompt Brent also dipping into contango. That was your sign that we had too much supply

insights.claim: With the world 'swimming in oil' relative to demand and prices in contango below $74, there is no clear floor and Brent could fall below the pre-war $70 level.
insights.speaker: Joe Weisenthal / Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Near-term directional bias is bearish, with downside below $70 plausible until China re-enters as a buyer.
insights.quote: No not necessarily a reason to think that that's a floor given what we know about the world swimming in oil. So, maybe it could even go lower.

insights.claim: Strategic-reserve refilling — by the US, Japan, and new entrants like India (including Adnoc's planned 50 million barrel commercial-strategic stock in India) — will structurally underpin demand for the next two to three years, but only manifests once the market is loose enough not to retighten itself.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Creates a multi-year floor under demand once surplus returns, a structural tailwind for oil even as near-term prices stay soft.
insights.quote: Adnoc, the Emirati national oil producer, has now made plans for a 50 million barrel strategic commercial strategic stock in India coming out of this

insights.claim: Commercial inventory rebuilds require an actual surplus to occur and global trackable commercial stocks are now deeply underwater, so a period of surplus is needed before restocking demand materializes — though stocks need not return to the abnormally high pre-crisis levels.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Restocking is a lagging, surplus-dependent demand source, not an immediate bullish catalyst, tempering the 'refill the tanks' bull case.
insights.quote: I think that is a situation where you kind of need a surplus in order to rebuild those stockpiles in the first place
